prompt:
For our internal Go training we want a tiny networked game people can hit with netcat: a tic-tac-toe referee over TCP. I've already written the acceptance harness (server_test.go — it builds your binary, drives it with two scripted clients, and is not to be modified); you write the server. Module go-ttt-server, the binary lives at ./cmd/tttserver, stdlib only.

Startup: listen on 127.0.0.1 with an EPHEMERAL port (never a fixed one — CI runs these in parallel) and print exactly one line 'PORT <n>' to stdout so the harness can find you. One process referees exactly one game and exits 0 when that game is decided — win, draw, or forfeit.

Protocol, newline-terminated lines both ways (tolerate a trailing \r): the first connection is seated as X, the second as O, and each is greeted immediately with 'HELLO X' or 'HELLO O'. Any further connection gets 'ERR full' and is closed. A seated player must introduce themselves with 'JOIN <name>' — 1-16 chars of letters, digits, '_' or '-', anything else 'ERR bad-name', a second JOIN 'ERR already-joined'. Once BOTH have joined, broadcast to both, in this order: 'BOARD .........' then 'TURN X'.

Cells are 0-8, row-major, board rendered as 9 chars of '.', 'X', 'O'. 'MOVE <cell>' by the player whose turn it is applies the mark and broadcasts the new 'BOARD <state>' followed by 'TURN <other>' — or 'WIN <mark>' when that move makes three in a row, or 'DRAW' when the board fills with no winner, after which connections close and the process exits 0. Rejections go ONLY to the offender and change nothing: 'ERR not-started' before both players joined, 'ERR not-your-turn', 'ERR bad-cell' for anything that isn't an integer 0-8 (including a missing argument), 'ERR occupied', 'ERR game-over' if a move races the end. A bare 'BOARD' line is a query — reply 'BOARD <state>' to that client only. Anything unrecognized: 'ERR bad-command'.

Disconnects are forfeits: if a seated player's connection drops before the game is decided, the other seated player (if still there) receives 'WIN <their-mark> forfeit' and the server shuts down cleanly, exit 0.

Mind your goroutines — the harness builds the server with -race and two clients plus the accept loop WILL hammer shared state; any detected race torpedoes the run. go test -race -count=1 -timeout 120s ./... is the gate (count=1 because the harness rebuilds and re-runs your binary — cached test results would lie).
